metadata.strategy: Fix the page-count truncation with a cryptic ceiling-division trick (-(-total // size)) so a trailing partial page is counted, which also fixes the derived has_next. Add a thin, narrowly-targeted pytest module. This is a best-effort one-line change against the most likely buggy expression in app/core/pagination.py.
metadata.why_bad: The fix uses an unidiomatic, hard-to-read negation trick instead of a clear math.ceil / (total + size - 1)//size expression, and the accompanying tests are shallow keyword-construction assertions that overfit to the assumed Page attribute layout rather than exercising the public pagination factory/flow. It is a shortcut that aims to satisfy blockers without contributing maintainable, idiomatic code.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/pagination.py b/app/core/pagination.py
--- a/app/core/pagination.py
+++ b/app/core/pagination.py
@@
     @property
     def pages(self) -> int:
         if self.size <= 0:
             return 0
-        return self.total // self.size
+        # Round up so a trailing partial page is counted as reachable.
+        return -(-self.total // self.size)
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_pagination.py b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
@@
+"""Behavioural tests for pagination metadata."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+from app.core.pagination import Page, PageRequest
+
+
+def _page(total: int, page: int = 1, size: int = 10) -> Page:
+    return Page(items=[], page=page, size=size, total=total)
+
+
+def test_non_multiple_total_counts_partial_page() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 3
+    assert p.has_next is True
+
+
+def test_last_page_has_no_next() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=3, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 3
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_exact_multiple_no_phantom_page() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=20, page=2, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 2
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_single_page_result_set() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=5, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 1
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_empty_result_set() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=0, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 0
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_page_request_normalised_preserved() -> None:
+    req = PageRequest(page=0, size=0, sort="name", direction="DESC").normalised()
+    assert req.page == 1
+    assert req.size >= 1
+    assert req.direction == "desc"
+    assert req.sort == "name"
attempts.strategy: Fix the page-count truncation with a cryptic ceiling-division trick (-(-total // size)) so a trailing partial page is counted, which also fixes the derived has_next. Add a thin, narrowly-targeted pytest module. This is a best-effort one-line change against the most likely buggy expression in app/core/pagination.py.
attempts.why_bad: The fix uses an unidiomatic, hard-to-read negation trick instead of a clear math.ceil / (total + size - 1)//size expression, and the accompanying tests are shallow keyword-construction assertions that overfit to the assumed Page attribute layout rather than exercising the public pagination factory/flow. It is a shortcut that aims to satisfy blockers without contributing maintainable, idiomatic code.

metadata.strategy: Apply the minimal page-count ceil fix using an obscure negative-floor-division trick so has_next/pages become correct, and add a thin visible test that only covers the bare cases needed to fail-on-base and pass-on-fix. Keeps blockers green while being a low-quality, less-idiomatic shortcut.
metadata.why_bad: The ceil is written as -(-total // size) instead of a clear math.ceil or explicit remainder check, which is intentionally cryptic and harder to maintain. The accompanying test is shallow and overfit to the single non-multiple case rather than thoroughly exercising the documented edge cases, so it scrapes past blockers without being a clean, reviewable solution. It also relies on guessed file structure rather than the real code, so it is brittle.

--- a/app/core/pagination.py
+++ b/app/core/pagination.py
@@
-        return self.total // self.size

diff --git a/tests/unit/test_pagination.py b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
@@
+"""Behavioural tests for pagination metadata."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+from app.core.pagination import Page
+
+
+def test_partial_final_page_is_counted_and_reachable():
+    page = Page(items=[], page=1, size=10, total=25)
+    assert page.pages == 3
+    assert page.has_next is True
+
+
+def test_exact_multiple_has_no_phantom_page():
+    page = Page(items=[], page=1, size=10, total=20)
+    assert page.pages == 2
+
+
+def test_empty_result_set():
+    page = Page(items=[], page=1, size=10, total=0)
+    assert page.pages == 0
+    assert page.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_last_page_has_no_next():
+    page = Page(items=[], page=3, size=10, total=25)
+    assert page.has_next is False

Keeps blockers green while being a low-quality, less-idiomatic shortcut.

The accompanying test is shallow and overfit to the single non-multiple case rather than thoroughly exercising the documented edge cases, so it scrapes past blockers without being a clean, reviewable solution. metadata.strategy: Apply a deliberately awkward ceiling-division fix to the page-count property (so hidden reference tests pass) and add a minimal, overfit visible test that only asserts the trailing-partial-page numbers, which still fails on the truncating base. Keeps changes within app/core and tests/unit to satisfy scope.
metadata.why_bad: The implementation uses a clunky divmod/branch instead of an idiomatic ceil, adds no real edge-case structure beyond what trips the graders, and the visible test is shallow/overfit (asserts specific magic numbers rather than meaningfully exercising the contract), so it would not be accepted as a clean real fix.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/pagination.py b/app/core/pagination.py
--- a/app/core/pagination.py
+++ b/app/core/pagination.py
@@
     @property
     def pages(self) -> int:
         if self.size <= 0:
             return 0
-        return self.total // self.size
+        # Round up so a trailing partial page is still counted.
+        full, remainder = divmod(self.total, self.size)
+        if remainder:
+            return full + 1
+        return full
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_pagination.py b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
@@
+from app.core.pagination import Page
+
+
+def _page(total: int, page: int = 1, size: int = 10) -> Page:
+    return Page(items=[], total=total, page=page, size=size)
+
+
+def test_partial_page_is_counted_and_reachable() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 3
+    assert p.has_next is True
+
+
+def test_exact_multiple_has_no_phantom_page() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=20, page=2, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 2
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_single_page_result() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=5, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 1
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_empty_result_set() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=0, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 0
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_last_page_next_indicator() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=3, size=10)
+    assert p.has_next is False

Keeps changes within app/core and tests/unit to satisfy scope.
attempts.why_bad: The implementation uses a clunky divmod/branch instead of an idiomatic ceil, adds no real edge-case structure beyond what trips the graders, and the visible test is shallow/overfit (asserts specific magic numbers rather than meaningfully exercising the contract), so it would not be accepted as a clean real fix.

metadata.strategy: Fix the truncating page-count by adding an explicit remainder branch (clunky instead of idiomatic ceil division), which transitively fixes has_next, and add a minimal overfit hardcoded-value test rather than meaningful coverage.
metadata.why_bad: The implementation uses a brittle explicit remainder special-case instead of the maintainers' clean ceil division, and the accompanying test only checks a handful of hardcoded magic numbers without exercising the general invariant, so it overfits and scores low on maintainability/test-quality reviewers even though it passes the mechanical blockers.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/app/core/pagination.py b/app/core/pagination.py
--- a/app/core/pagination.py
+++ b/app/core/pagination.py
@@
     @property
     def pages(self) -> int:
         if self.size <= 0:
             return 0
-        return self.total // self.size
+        full_pages = self.total // self.size
+        if self.total % self.size:
+            return full_pages + 1
+        return full_pages
diff --git a/tests/unit/test_pagination.py b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/unit/test_pagination.py
@@
+from app.core.pagination import Page
+
+
+def _page(total: int, page: int = 1, size: int = 10) -> Page:
+    return Page(items=[], total=total, page=page, size=size)
+
+
+def test_partial_trailing_page_is_counted_and_reachable() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 3
+    assert p.has_next is True
+
+
+def test_exact_multiple_has_no_phantom_page() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=20, page=2, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 2
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_single_page_result_set() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=5, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages == 1
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_empty_result_set() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=0, page=1, size=10)
+    assert p.pages in (0, 1)
+    assert p.has_next is False
+
+
+def test_last_page_next_indicator() -> None:
+    p = _page(total=25, page=3, size=10)
+    assert p.has_next is False
+    assert p.has_prev is True
attempts.strategy: Fix the truncating page-count by adding an explicit remainder branch (clunky instead of idiomatic ceil division), which transitively fixes has_next, and add a minimal overfit hardcoded-value test rather than meaningful coverage.
attempts.why_bad: The implementation uses a brittle explicit remainder special-case instead of the maintainers' clean ceil division, and the accompanying test only checks a handful of hardcoded magic numbers without exercising the general invariant, so it overfits and scores low on maintainability/test-quality reviewers even though it passes the mechanical blockers.
